section.title: The Altar and the Curses
section.sectionRationale: This chapter enacts a covenant renewal ceremony at Shechem: the law written on plastered stones, an altar of unhewn stone for atonement, and twelve curses recited antiphonally between Mount Gerizim and Mount Ebal, each sealed by Israel's own binding 'Amen,' showing the nation publicly agreeing that these curses are the just due of the sins named.
section.thesis: Before entering the land, Israel must publicly inscribe the law, build an altar of unworked stone, and recite twelve curses against specific hidden and social sins, each answered by the whole nation's 'Amen,' binding Israel to acknowledge that breaking the covenant justly brings God's curse, the very curses chapter 28 will describe in full and Christ will one day bear.
section.frameNote: Four movements: writing the law on large plastered stones; building an altar of uncut stones for burnt and peace offerings; the tribes divided between Mount Gerizim for blessing and Mount Ebal for curse; and the Levites' recitation of twelve curses, each sealed by the people's own 'Amen'.

units.label: The law written in stone
units.note: plain to see, for all to read

verses.text: Then Moses and the elders of Israel commanded the people: "Keep all the commandments I am giving you today.
And on the day you cross the Jordan into the land that the LORD your God is giving you, set up large stones and coat them with plaster.

Write on them all the words of this law when you have crossed over to enter the land that the LORD your God is giving you, a land flowing with milk and honey, just as the LORD, the God of your fathers, has promised you.
And when you have crossed the Jordan, you are to set up these stones on Mount Ebal, as I am commanding you today, and you are to coat them with plaster.
Moreover, you are to build there an {{mizbeach|altar}} to the LORD your God, an altar of stones. You must not use any iron tool on them.
You shall build the altar of the LORD your God with uncut stones and offer upon it burnt offerings to the LORD your God.
There you are to sacrifice your peace offerings, eating them and rejoicing in the presence of the LORD your God.
And you shall write distinctly upon these stones all the words of this law."

units.label: This day you have become the LORD's people

verses.text: Then Moses and the Levitical priests spoke to all Israel: "Be silent, O Israel, and listen! This day you have become the people of the LORD your God.
You shall therefore obey the voice of the LORD your God and follow His commandments and statutes I am giving you today."

units.label: Blessing from Gerizim, curse from Ebal

verses.text: On that day Moses commanded the people:
"When you have crossed the Jordan, these tribes shall stand on Mount Gerizim to bless the people: Simeon, Levi, Judah, Issachar, Joseph, and Benjamin.
And these tribes shall stand on Mount Ebal to deliver the curse: Reuben, Gad, Asher, Zebulun, Dan, and Naphtali.

units.label: Twelve curses, and Israel's Amen
units.note: the nation agrees to the law's justice

verses.text: Then the Levites shall proclaim in a loud voice to every Israelite:

'{{arur|Cursed}} is the man who makes a carved idol or molten image, an abomination to the LORD, the work of the hands of a craftsman, and sets it up in secret.' And let all the people say, '{{amen|Amen}}!'
'Cursed is he who dishonors his father or mother.' And let all the people say, 'Amen!'
'Cursed is he who moves his neighbor's boundary stone.' And let all the people say, 'Amen!'
'Cursed is he who lets a blind man wander in the road.' And let all the people say, 'Amen!'
'Cursed is he who withholds justice from the foreigner, the fatherless, or the widow.' And let all the people say, 'Amen!'
'Cursed is he who sleeps with his father's wife, for he has violated his father's marriage bed.' And let all the people say, 'Amen!'
'Cursed is he who lies with any animal.' And let all the people say, 'Amen!'
'Cursed is he who sleeps with his sister, the daughter of his father or the daughter of his mother.' And let all the people say, 'Amen!'
'Cursed is he who sleeps with his mother-in-law.' And let all the people say, 'Amen!'
'Cursed is he who strikes down his neighbor in secret.' And let all the people say, 'Amen!'
'Cursed is he who accepts a bribe to kill an innocent person.' And let all the people say, 'Amen!'
'Cursed is he who does not put the words of this law into practice.' And let all the people say, 'Amen!'

arur.gloss: Cursed. The recurring refrain 'cursed is...' pronounced by the Levites over specific sins, met each time by the whole people's binding 'Amen,' shows Israel publicly agreeing that these curses are the just due of the sin named.

amen.gloss: Truly, so be it, from a root meaning firm or faithful. Israel's repeated response binds the whole nation to agree that these curses justly apply to whoever commits these sins, including, ultimately, themselves.

mizbeach.gloss: Altar, from a root meaning to slaughter or sacrifice. Built here of uncut, natural stones untouched by human tool, so that the place of atonement owes nothing to human craft or achievement.

basic.context: Before crossing into the land, Israel is to hold a covenant renewal ceremony at Shechem: writing the law on large plastered stones for all to read, building an altar of natural, unhewn stones for offerings, dividing six tribes to Mount Gerizim for blessing and six to Mount Ebal for curse, and having the Levites recite twelve specific curses against hidden sins and social injustice, each one sealed by the whole nation's own spoken 'Amen.'
basic.hermeneutics: This is a real, one-time covenant ceremony for Israel's specific entry into the land, historically situated at Shechem between two actual mountains; its enduring message, however, that God's people publicly and knowingly agree to the justice of His curse against sin, sets up the whole logic Paul later uses in Galatians 3:10-13 to explain why Christ had to bear the law's curse.
basic.originalLanguages: Each curse begins arur, cursed, and the people answer amen, truly, so be it, literally agreeing that the curse is deserved. The mizbeach, altar, must be built of uncut stones, so that atonement owes nothing to human craftsmanship or achievement.
basic.typology: Israel's own spoken agreement to the law's curses anticipates Paul's argument in Galatians 3:10, that everyone who does not keep the whole law is under a curse, a curse Christ Himself takes on at the cross, becoming a curse for us, Galatians 3:13, quoting Deuteronomy's own language of hanging on a tree.
basic.christ: Galatians 3:10-13 draws directly on this covenant curse logic: since no one keeps the whole law perfectly, everyone stands under its curse, but Christ 'redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us,' so that the blessing promised to Abraham might reach the nations.
basic.god: God requires His people to know and own the seriousness of covenant unfaithfulness before they even enter the land, not surprising them later; He is scrupulously fair, requiring Israel's own informed 'Amen' to the justice of the curse before ever executing it.
basic.crossReferences: Galatians 3:10-13 draws directly on the logic of curse and blessing established here. Joshua 8:30-35 records Israel actually carrying out this ceremony at Mount Ebal after entering the land. Nehemiah 10:29 shows Israel later binding themselves again with a curse and an oath to keep the law. Revelation 22:3 promises 'no longer will there be any curse,' the final undoing of what is set up here.
basic.oneStory: Israel publicly agrees that breaking covenant deserves God's curse, setting up the entire logic of blessing and curse that chapter 28 will unfold in detail, and that the gospel will finally resolve when Christ bears the curse His people had already agreed they deserved.

context.literary: This chapter opens the covenant renewal section running through chapter 30, moving from case law to the solemn ceremony that will frame the blessings and curses of chapter 28 and the covenant of chapter 29.
context.historical: Mount Gerizim and Mount Ebal flank the ancient city of Shechem, a site already significant in patriarchal history, Genesis 12:6-7 and 33:18-20; Joshua later carries out this exact ceremony after Israel enters the land, Joshua 8:30-35.
context.canonical: This covenant curse ceremony becomes the direct background for Paul's argument in Galatians about the law's curse and Christ's redemption, one of the New Testament's clearest uses of Deuteronomy's covenant theology.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To have Israel publicly and knowingly commit itself, before entering the land, to the covenant's blessings and curses, ensuring the nation cannot later claim ignorance of the seriousness of covenant unfaithfulness.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The specific ceremony, the stones, the altar, the two mountains, describes a one-time historical event for Israel's covenant entry into the land; its underlying principle, that God's people must know and agree to the real consequences of covenant unfaithfulness, and that the law's curse is genuinely deserved by all who break it, remains foundational for understanding the gospel, as Paul demonstrates in Galatians 3.

debates.question: Why does the list of curses in verses 15-26 focus especially on hidden sins?

views.label: Because human courts cannot try what only God can see
views.case: Most of these curses, secret idolatry, moving a boundary marker at night, secretly striking a neighbor, bribery, describe sins easily hidden from human courts, so the curse invokes God's own certain judgment where human justice cannot reach.

views.label: Because the whole list functions as a summary of the law's basic demands
views.case: Ending with 'cursed is he who does not put the words of this law into practice,' verse 26, the list may function as a representative summary of covenant obedience as a whole, not merely a catalogue of undetectable crimes.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree the final curse, verse 26, generalizes the whole law's claim on Israel, which Paul quotes directly in Galatians 3:10 to show that no one is justified by law-keeping alone.

study.originalLanguages: Each curse begins arur, cursed, verses 15-26, and the people answer amen, truly, so be it, agreeing the curse is deserved. The mizbeach, altar, verse 5, must be built of uncut stones, so that atonement owes nothing to human craftsmanship.

typology.type: Israel's spoken 'Amen' to the law's curse
typology.fulfillment: Paul's argument that all who rely on the law are under its curse, Galatians 3:10.
Christ's atonement, which likewise owes nothing to human achievement or craft, but is entirely God's own provision.

study.christ: Galatians 3:10-13 draws directly on this covenant curse logic: since no one keeps the whole law perfectly, everyone stands under its curse, but Christ 'redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us,' so that the blessing promised to Abraham might reach the nations through faith.
study.god: God requires His people to know and own the seriousness of covenant unfaithfulness before they even enter the land, not surprising them later; He is scrupulously fair, requiring Israel's own informed 'Amen' to the justice of the curse before it is ever executed.

crossReferences.note: Draws directly on the logic of curse and blessing established here.
Records Israel actually carrying out this ceremony at Mount Ebal after entering the land.
Shows Israel later binding themselves again with a curse and an oath to keep the law.
Promises 'no longer will there be any curse,' the final undoing of what is set up here.

study.oneStory: Israel publicly agrees that breaking covenant deserves God's curse, setting up the entire logic of blessing and curse that chapter 28 will unfold in detail, and that the gospel will finally resolve when Christ bears the curse His people had already agreed they deserved.

quiz.q: What were the Israelites commanded to write on large stones coated with plaster, verses 2-3?

options.text: All the words of this law.
options.note: Verses 3 and 8 state this explicitly.

options.text: The names of the twelve tribes only.
options.note: The text specifies the words of the law, not merely tribal names.

options.text: A list of Canaanite cities to conquer.
options.note: The text specifies the law, not a military list.

options.text: Nothing; no writing is commanded.
options.note: Verses 2-3 and 8 give an explicit command.

quiz.q: What kind of stones must the altar be built from, verses 5-6?

options.text: Uncut, natural stones; no iron tool may be used on them.
options.note: Verses 5-6 state this explicitly.

options.text: Finely carved and polished stones.
options.note: The text explicitly forbids using any iron tool to cut or shape the stones.

options.text: Stones brought from Egypt.
options.note: The text specifies natural, unhewn stones with no reference to their origin.

options.text: No instruction is given about the altar's stones.
options.note: Verses 5-6 are explicit.

quiz.q: Which six tribes stood on Mount Gerizim to pronounce the blessing, verse 12?

options.text: Simeon, Levi, Judah, Issachar, Joseph, and Benjamin.
options.note: Verse 12 names these tribes explicitly.

options.text: Reuben, Gad, Asher, Zebulun, Dan, and Naphtali.
options.note: These six tribes instead stood on Mount Ebal for the curse, verse 13.

options.text: All twelve tribes stood together on Gerizim.
options.note: The text explicitly divides the tribes between two mountains.

options.text: No tribes are named for this ceremony.
options.note: Verses 12-13 name all twelve tribes across the two mountains.

quiz.q: What did the people say after each curse was pronounced by the Levites?

options.text: 'Amen!'
options.note: This response follows every curse from verse 15 through verse 26.

options.text: Nothing; they remained silent.
options.note: The text explicitly records the people's spoken response each time.

options.text: 'We refuse this curse.'
options.note: The text records agreement, 'Amen,' not refusal.

options.text: No response is recorded.
options.note: Every curse is followed by the people's explicit 'Amen.'

quiz.q: What does the final curse in verse 26 declare?

options.text: Cursed is he who does not put the words of this law into practice.
options.note: Verse 26 states this explicit, summarizing curse, later quoted by Paul in Galatians 3:10.

options.text: Cursed is he who lives outside the land of Israel.
options.note: The final curse concerns obedience to the law, not geography.

options.text: Cursed is he who is born a foreigner.
options.note: The final curse concerns disobedience to the law, not ethnicity.

options.text: No final, summarizing curse is given.
options.note: Verse 26 gives an explicit, comprehensive final curse.

questions: Israel had to write the law in plain sight and publicly agree, 'Amen,' to the justice of its curses. Where do you need to be more honest with yourself about what God's word actually requires, rather than keeping it vague?

The altar was built of unworked stone, so atonement owed nothing to human skill. Where are you tempted to think your own effort or achievement earns you standing before God, rather than resting in what Christ has done?
Many of the curses in this chapter target sins done in secret, moving a boundary marker, a hidden bribe, a secret attack. What sin are you hiding that you assume no one, including God, can see?
Christ became a curse for us so that the blessing of Abraham might come to us. How does knowing Jesus already bore the exact curse you deserved change the weight of guilt you carry today?
Israel's whole nation answered 'Amen' together, publicly agreeing to the same standard. Who in your life could you agree with, out loud, to pursue faithfulness to God together?

translationNotes.note: Some ancient manuscript traditions read 'Mount Gerizim' rather than 'Mount Ebal' for the location of the stones and altar; the Masoretic Hebrew text followed by the BSB reads Ebal, consistent with Ebal being the mountain of curse in verse 13.

The list of twelve curses is not exhaustive of the whole law but functions representatively, especially highlighting sins easily hidden from human courts, culminating in the sweeping final curse of verse 26 quoted by Paul in Galatians 3:10.
Paul quotes this verse in Galatians 3:10 to argue that everyone who relies on keeping the law is under its curse, since no one keeps 'everything written in the Book of the Law' perfectly.
